title: His Dark Materials — The Daemon Visible
subtitle: The daemon as the visible soul, the alethiometer as developed phantasia, the amber spyglass as tattva-vision, and the Republic of Heaven as the work itself

summary.one_line: Pullman's trilogy is the most literal rendering of the daimon in modern literature: the daemon as the visible soul, the alethiometer as the developed phantasia (the image-reading faculty), the ambe...
summary.scope: Covers the entire His Dark Materials trilogy (Northern Lights / The Golden Compass, The Subtle Knife, The Amber Spyglass), with primary focus on the daemon-body relationship, the alethiometer as a noetic instrument, the subtle knife as the power of discrimination, and the theological architecture of the Authority vs. the Republic of Heaven.

sources.label: Pullman, Philip — Northern Lights / The Golden Compass (1995)

body.text: [See HDM Northern Lights ch.1-7 and throughout. Pullman's daemon is the most literal rendering in modern literature of the Platonic daimon as the visible soul. Each human has a daemon — the 'outer self' that walks beside them in animal form, that speaks in a voice that is theirs and not theirs, that knows things they do not consciously know. Pan (Pantalaimon) is not a pet or a symbol but Lyra's soul made visible, tactile, separable. The body and the daemon are one continuum in two modes: the physical body and the subtle body side by side. When Pan settles into a fixed form at puberty, this is the crystallisation of the soul's character — the moment the daimon's form becomes the dharma of the person. It is the exact inverse of the Western occult maxim 'know thyself': here the self is known by looking beside you.]

[See HDM Northern Lights and throughout. The alethiometer (the 'truth-measurer') is the developed phantasia — the image-reading faculty of the soul that perceives meaning directly through symbols. It has 36 symbols arranged around a face, each bearing an infinite web of meanings. The alethiometer does not 'tell' answers; it presents correspondences that the reader must interpret. This is the exact function of the Neoplatonic phantasia: the middle faculty that receives noetic impressions and translates them into images the discursive mind can approach. Lyra reads the alethiometer by instinct — she does not 'learn' it but remembers it — because the capacity is innate to the soul before the dust of convention settles. Her loss of the ability and later recovery is the arc of the soul forgetting and remembering its own nature.]

[See HDM Amber Spyglass ch.27-30. The amber spyglass built by Mary Malone is the instrument that sees Dust directly — the 'amber' of the title referring to the golden colour of the particles of consciousness. The spyglass functions by polarising light through amber (a fossilised resin, itself a substance that preserves life across time) to reveal what is always present but invisible to ordinary sight. This is the capacity to see the tattvas — the 'thatnesses' or real categories of reality — directly, without the mediation of concept. Mary sees Dust as a continuous golden rain, the substrate of consciousness itself. The amber spyglass is the purified vision that perceives the subtle realm as directly as the physical: the vaikharī level rendered transparent to the madhyama.]

[See HDM Amber Spyglass ch.32-33. The separation of Lyra and Pantalaimon by the harpies in the land of the dead is the breach of the subtle body — the traumatic division of the soul from itself. The severance is agony because the daemon and the body have never been apart; their unity is the ground of Lyra's existence. Pan, pulled away across the lake of the dead, becomes a distant agonised shape. Lyra experiences this as the tearing of her own interior. This is the Śaiva description of the soul in the state of extreme impurity (āṇavamala): the sense of separation so radical that the soul believes itself to be only the body, having lost the daimon. The reunion — when Lyra steps out of the land of the dead and Pan flies back to her — is the restoration of the subtle body, the reintegration without which no further work is possible.]

[See HDM Amber Spyglass ch.28-29. The Authority — the ancient being who claims to be God — is revealed as the first angel to crystallise out of Dust, who then claimed to be the creator. He is not Śiva but the false Śiva: the hypostatised ego of the cosmos that mistakes its seniority for supremacy. By the time we meet him, he is a decrepit figure in a crystal box, 'demented' and 'powerless,' kept alive by the Regent Metatron. This is the gnostic Demiurge — the limited creator who believes he is the Absolute. The death of the Authority is not a murder but a dissolution: he simply dissipates when released from the crystal, 'as if he were a bubble of air.' The false god was never a god at all — he was the first and most subtle contraction.]

[See HDM Amber Spyglass ch.36 and throughout. The Republic of Heaven is Pullman's answer to the Kingdom of Heaven — not a place but a project, not a reward but a practice. 'The Kingdom of Heaven is a state of — a state of — what? It's a state of happiness, I suppose. But the Republic of Heaven is a place where people work together for the good of all.' This is the work itself (sādhana) understood not as the attainment of a fixed state but as the continuous building of consciousness. Dust — the material of consciousness itself — increases when humans love, learn, create, and attend to one another. The Republic is not waiting to be found; it is being built with every act of attention, every moment of love, every deliberate choice to see clearly. The work is the goal; the path is the destination.]

gaps: Full typology of daemon forms and their correlations with human types needs extraction
Detailed symbol-by-symbol analysis of the 36 alethiometer symbols missing
Deeper connection between Mary Malone's scientific method and tantric empiricism needed
The mulefa civilisation as a living example of tattva-dharma could be expanded

issues.title: All passages are meta-commentary — need primary text extraction
issues.description: Passages currently describe the esoteric mapping. Real passages should include actual quotations from the novels with analysis.

issues.title: The subtle knife as the power of discrimination (viveka)
issues.description: The subtle knife cuts windows between worlds — this is viveka, the discriminative faculty that sees through the apparent separateness of realms. The knife's double-edged danger (it cuts anything, including the wielder) is the risk of discrimination without integration. Not yet developed.

versions.summary: Initial RO created mapping His Dark Materials onto the daimon tradition and Tantric-Śaiva esoteric architecture. Six passages covering the daemon visible, alethiometer as phantasia, amber spyglass as tattva-vision, the separation of Lyra and Pan, the Authority as false Śiva, and the Republic of Heaven as the work.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with 3 primary sources and 6 passages
